stories.headline: Colorado's state budget shortfall grows to $1.5 billion, forcing deeper cuts
stories.summary:
Colorado's budget shortfall has ballooned to more than $1.5 billion, nonpartisan Capitol staff told lawmakers Thursday — roughly half a billion dollars worse than anticipated when the legislative session began in January. The Joint Budget Committee has been cutting spending for weeks, including reductions to Medicaid, which is the primary driver of the gap, but even those cuts leave a remaining deficit of about $1.47 billion.

The shortfall grew largely because nonpartisan staff now expect tax revenues in the current fiscal year, ending June 30, to come in about $350 million lower than previously projected. Combined with a jump in Medicaid costs since the last session, the total deficit grew by roughly $650 million relative to September projections.

The governor's office and legislative analysts disagreed sharply on revenue forecasts, with the Polis administration projecting nearly $700 million more in individual income tax collections than the nonpartisan Legislative Council Staff. Much of the uncertainty centers on the downstream effects of the federal H.R. 1 tax and spending law, which is tied to Colorado's state tax code.

'Today's economic forecast is nothing short of devastating,' said state Sen. Jeff Bridges, a Greenwood Village Democrat and vice chair of the budget committee. The JBC had been scheduled to finalize its draft budget next week, a timeline that is now almost certainly impossible.

stories.headline: Trump renews push to free Tina Peters as Gov. Polis weighs clemency
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump on Wednesday again called for the release of Tina Peters, the former Mesa County election clerk serving a nine-year prison sentence after her August 2024 conviction on seven charges, including four felonies, related to a 2021 breach of the county's voting systems. Trump posted on Truth Social calling Peters a '73-year-old woman with cancer' given a 'death sentence,' and demanding Gov. Jared Polis free her.

Polis has acknowledged the sentence appears harsh given Peters had no prior criminal record and has noted a sentencing disparity — a former state lawmaker convicted of the same offense received only probation and community service. The governor has said he is considering clemency but has made clear that any successful application would require Peters to demonstrate genuine remorse, something officials say she has not shown.

Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser, whose office helped prosecute Peters, emphasized that clemency should be based on remorse and rehabilitation, not political pressure. U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet, also a Democratic candidate for governor, similarly opposed a pardon or commutation, saying surrendering to Trump's pressure would not make Colorado stronger or safer.

Trump previously announced a federal pardon for Peters, but such a move has no effect on her state conviction, as only the governor has the authority to grant clemency in state cases.

stories.headline: Denver auditor calls Johnston's homelessness program 'poorly planned,' disputes $20 million in costs
stories.summary:
Denver Auditor Tim O'Brien released a critical report Thursday on Mayor Mike Johnston's All in Mile High homelessness initiative, characterizing it as 'poorly planned' and alleging that the program cost approximately $20 million more than the mayor's office reported to the City Council. According to the auditor, city records show expenses of roughly $178 million over the program's first two years, while the mayor's office told the council the total was about $158 million.

Johnston's office pushed back sharply, calling the audit report misleading and saying the discrepancy arose because auditors included costs — such as cold-weather shelters opened under prior administrations — that city officials did not consider part of All in Mile High. 'Not a single dollar was overspent, not a single dollar is not accounted for,' Johnston told The Denver Post.

The audit also raised concerns about how the city selected shelter locations, awarded some contracts, and reported outcomes for people in the program. The city's dashboard counts 3,530 moves of individuals into All in Mile High's noncongregate shelters since July 2023, though some involve the same person more than once; a broader measure credits Johnston's administration with moving about 7,300 people into more permanent housing.

Annual point-in-time counts showed street homelessness in Denver declined 45 percent between early 2023 and early 2025, though advocates dispute the reliability of those single-day snapshots. The dispute between the auditor and mayor's office, which has flared before, played out publicly Thursday at an Audit Committee meeting.

stories.headline: Man arrested on first-degree murder charge in Denver road-rage shooting in Montbello
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested Lavon Ellis Wedgeworth, 44, on suspicion of first-degree murder following a fatal road-rage shooting Tuesday in the city's Montbello neighborhood. Officers responded just before 11:45 a.m. to the 12400 block of East 42nd Avenue, near the Interstate 70 exit at Peoria Street, where they found a man who had been shot three times; he was transported to a hospital and pronounced dead shortly after noon.

Surveillance footage showed a gray Jeep Grand Cherokee with chrome wheels and a bike rack forcing the victim's vehicle to the side of the road before the shooting. Police located a matching Jeep several blocks away, registered to Wedgeworth, and obtained a search warrant that turned up fired cartridge casings matching those found at the scene, three handguns, loaded magazines, live ammunition, and suspected blood on the vehicle's interior and exterior.

An anonymous video sent to investigators also appeared to show Wedgeworth and another man fighting with the victim before Wedgeworth drew a gun and fired three shots at the victim, who had raised his hands. As of Thursday, Wedgeworth remained held at Denver's Downtown Detention Center with no bond set; the victim had not yet been publicly identified by the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner.

stories.headline: Explosion and fire at Denver gas station injure two, caused by broken Xcel natural gas line
stories.summary:
Two people were injured Thursday morning when an explosion and fire tore through a Shell fuel station and convenience store in Denver's Valverde neighborhood, located at 510 N. Bryant St. near the Sixth Avenue and Interstate 25 interchange. Denver Fire Department crews arrived at 11:29 a.m. to find the building fully engulfed, with debris scattered in the street from the blast.

Investigators determined the explosion was caused by a broken Xcel Energy natural gas line that allowed fumes to accumulate inside the store before igniting. Firefighters contained most of the flames within 15 minutes, but the fire was not fully extinguished until Xcel crews arrived hours later to clamp the ruptured line.

Both victims — who bystanders said were still inside when crews arrived, but had in fact already escaped — were transported by ambulance to Denver Health with moderate burn and blast injuries. Xcel Energy said no other customers were affected and the company is investigating the cause of the line break; fire officials said there are no ongoing hazardous materials concerns.

stories.headline: Denver shatters all-time March heat record at 85 degrees, more records expected through weekend
stories.summary:
Denver hit 85 degrees at Denver International Airport on Thursday, breaking the all-time record for the hottest March temperature ever recorded in the city, a mark that dates back to record-keeping that began in 1872. The previous monthly record of 84 degrees was set on March 26, 1971; Thursday's peak also broke the daily record for March 19, which had stood since 1907.

The National Weather Service forecast additional daily records Friday and Saturday, with Denver expected to reach 84 and 87 degrees respectively on those days. Several other Front Range cities, including Boulder, Fort Collins, and Greeley, are also forecast to break both daily and monthly records through the weekend, with the Eastern Plains potentially reaching 90 degrees by Saturday.

A Fire Weather Watch is in effect for most of Colorado on Saturday, with forecasters warning of winds of 30 to 35 mph combined with an extremely dry air mass that will elevate fire danger significantly. Temperatures are expected to drop into the 70s Sunday and Monday — still roughly 15 degrees above average for late March — before potentially rebounding into the 80s again the following week.

Climate scientists noted the event is consistent with human-caused climate change and warned that the heat will accelerate snowmelt during a year already marked by widespread snow drought across the region.

stories.headline: Update: Buttermilk wildfire near Westcliffe reaches 95% containment, mandatory evacuations lifted
stories.summary:
Custer County officials lifted all mandatory evacuation orders Thursday evening after firefighters reached 95 percent containment on the Buttermilk fire burning north of Westcliffe in southern Colorado. The 65-acre blaze, which had threatened nearly 100 structures, resulted in no injuries and no destroyed buildings; all road closures were lifted by 5:30 p.m.

The fire was caused Wednesday when an aspen tree fell onto a Sangre de Cristo Electric Association power line and an automatic system repeatedly tried to reconnect the power, sparking the blaze. Utility officials said the automatic reconnect feature will be disabled for the duration of fire season going forward.

Fire crews were set to remain on scene overnight and continue working toward full containment Friday. In an unusual footnote, 16 cows were also evacuated during the incident and housed at the Wet Mountain Valley Saddle Club.

Two additional fires were burning on Fort Carson land near Colorado Springs — one at 1,600 acres with 90 percent containment and a second at 65 acres with 40 percent containment. With approximately 85 percent of Colorado considered abnormally dry or worse, fire officials warned that dangerous conditions are expected to persist, particularly heading into the weekend.

stories.headline: RTD proposes reinstating C Line and launching new T Line in June service overhaul
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District has proposed reinstating the C Line light rail service and introducing an entirely new T Line as part of a package of service changes planned for June, the agency announced. RTD also proposed extending the R Line to RidgeGate Parkway Station, increasing train frequencies on the B and G lines, and improving service across 12 bus routes.

The changes are designed in part to support the next phase of RTD's multi-year Downtown Rail Reconstruction Project, which this year will involve work along the Colfax Avenue rail alignment and is expected to affect service on the H and D lines. The C Line was previously discontinued as part of prior service reductions.

The proposals come as RTD faces a previously reported $215 million budget deficit and has been weighing potential service cuts across the metro area. The June changes represent a mixed picture — restoring some capacity in certain corridors while reconstruction work disrupts others.

stories.headline: Stars edge Avalanche 2-1 in shootout, trimming Colorado's Central Division lead to two points
stories.summary:
The Dallas Stars defeated the Colorado Avalanche 2-1 in a shootout Thursday night at Ball Arena, cutting Colorado's lead in the Central Division standings to just two points. Despite controlling possession and generating the majority of offensive chances, the Avalanche were unable to convert, with Dallas goaltender Jake Oettinger stopping 33 of 34 shots in regulation and overtime.

Cale Makar scored on the power play in the first period for his 20th goal of the season, and Scott Wedgewood made 17 saves for Colorado. Jason Robertson tied the game for Dallas in regulation, and Wyatt Johnston scored the only goal of the shootout to secure the extra point for the Stars.

Head coach Jared Bednar expressed little concern about the result despite the playoff implications, citing the quality of his team's overall performance. 'I watch our team play like that and I have very little issues with the way we played,' Bednar said. 'When your team has an effort like that, you'd love for them to get rewarded, but it's not always the case.'

The loss keeps the pressure on Colorado as the regular season winds down, with Dallas closing the gap in a tight Central Division race.

stories.headline: Broncos sign safety Tycen Anderson, ending run as NFL's last team without outside free-agent addition
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos agreed to a one-year contract with free-agent safety Tycen Anderson on Thursday, ending their status as the only NFL team that had not signed a free agent from another club since the start of the new league year. Anderson, 26, spent four seasons with the Cincinnati Bengals after being selected in the fifth round of the 2022 NFL Draft out of Toledo.

Anderson is primarily a special-teams contributor, playing 83 percent of the Bengals' special-teams snaps last season and ranking ninth among 68 safeties in Pro Football Focus's special-teams grade. He totaled 23 tackles in 17 games in 2025 while also serving as a rotational backup safety.

The signing comes one day after reports emerged that Anderson had verbally agreed to terms, and follows the Broncos' high-profile trade for Miami Dolphins wide receiver Jaylen Waddle earlier in the week. Denver had previously re-signed 17 in-house free agents this offseason before making Anderson their first outside addition.

Anderson's arrival gives Denver three of the top 11 safeties in special-teams grading, joining Devon Key and JL Skinner, reinforcing a phase of the game the team has prioritized under its current roster-building approach.

stories.headline: Sportico values Broncos at $6.55 billion, Nuggets at $4.8 billion in latest franchise rankings
stories.summary:
Sportico released its latest franchise valuations across the five major American sports leagues this week, with the Denver Broncos coming in as the metro area's most valuable team at $6.55 billion — a significant increase from the roughly $4.65 billion the Walton-Penner ownership group paid to acquire the team in August 2022. The Broncos ranked 22nd overall among all franchises and 15th in the NFL.

The Denver Nuggets were valued at $4.8 billion, ranking 53rd overall and 17th in the NBA. The Colorado Avalanche came in at $1.81 billion (103rd overall, 20th in the NHL), the Colorado Rockies at $1.68 billion (109th overall, 24th in MLB), and the Colorado Rapids at $480 million (152nd overall, 28th in MLS).

The valuations reflect the continued appreciation of professional sports franchises across all major American leagues, with Denver's teams seeing growth across the board since their most recent ownership changes and transactions.

stories.headline: 3,800 JBS workers strike at Greeley beef plant over wages and healthcare
stories.summary:
Approximately 3,800 workers at the JBS beef processing plant in Greeley went on strike over stalled contract negotiations involving wages and healthcare benefits. The Greeley facility is one of the largest beef processing operations in the United States, and the work stoppage has the potential to affect beef supply chains regionally and nationally.

The strike reflects broader tensions in the meatpacking industry over compensation and working conditions. JBS, a Brazilian-owned multinational that is among the world's largest meat processors, has faced labor disputes at various facilities in recent years.

Greeley's JBS plant is a major employer in Weld County and a significant economic driver for the northern Front Range. The outcome of negotiations could have lasting implications for workers and the regional economy.

weather.notable: About 30 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I'll keep all my emotions right here, and then one day I'll die.

weekendEvents.title: Denver March Powwow
weekendEvents.description: One of the largest American Indian powwows in the country, celebrating the heritage and culture of American Indian communities. The 50th annual gathering features dancers, drummers, and traditional arts.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Dragon Boat Film Festival
weekendEvents.description: Colorado's only Asian and Asian American film festival, showcasing the culture, contributions and accomplishments of Asian and Asian Pacific Islander communities through film. This year's theme is 'Threads of Connection,' with the festival opening with a screening of The Girl Who Stole Time. Tickets start at $16.
weekendEvents.location: Sie FilmCenter, 2510 E Colfax Ave., Denver

weekendEvents.title: World's Largest Dinosaurs — Opening Weekend
weekendEvents.description: A traveling exhibition organized by the American Museum of Natural History that opens this weekend at the Denver Museum of Nature and Science. Visitors can walk alongside a life-size 60-foot-long Mamenchisaurus model and explore interactive stations on sauropod biology. Adult admission $9, youth/senior $7.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Museum of Nature and Science, 2001 Colorado Blvd., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Glowing Wild at the Denver Zoo
weekendEvents.description: An after-dark event at the Denver Zoo featuring more than 60 illuminated scenes and 175 large-scale lanterns handcrafted by Chinese artisans, depicting animals and conservation stories. Tickets start at $19 for youth ages 3–15; adults $27.95; children 2 and under free.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance, 2300 Steele St., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Spring Art Walk at the Denver Milk Market
weekendEvents.description: A free outdoor art event featuring more than a dozen local artists selling original work, including pottery, jewelry, handcrafted tote bags, and larger one-of-a-kind pieces.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Milk Market, 1800 Wazee St., Suite 100, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Playground Ensemble 20-Hour Marathon
weekendEvents.description: In honor of its twentieth anniversary, Denver's Playground Ensemble is staging a 20-hour marathon of music performances across four venues. The event is divided into four blocks; all but the evening concerts are free.
weekendEvents.location: Four Denver venues — see event listing for details

weekendEvents.title: Denver Public Library Used Book Sale
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Public Library Friends Foundation hosts its spring used book sale with books, CDs, DVDs, and more available for purchase. On Sunday, shoppers can fill a bag for $15; bring your own bag.
weekendEvents.time: Friday 10 a.m.–4:30 p.m.; Saturday 11 a.m.–4:30 p.m.; Sunday 10 a.m.–3 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: Central Library, 10 West 14th Avenue Parkway, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Free Day at the CELL (Counter Extremism Learning Lab)
weekendEvents.description: Thanks to SCFD funding, admission is free Friday at this interactive exhibit focused on national security issues and how the public can play a role in enhancing public safety.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Cat Fest
weekendEvents.description: A feline-themed event in Loveland featuring kitten yoga, a cat show, cat-themed vendors and crafts, adoption booths, and more. General admission $20; VIP $40; children 5 and under free.
weekendEvents.location: The Ranch Events Complex, 5280 Arena Circle, Loveland

weekendEvents.title: Joyride Brewing 5th Annual Winter Luau
weekendEvents.description: Joyride Brewing's fifth annual winter luau brings tropical vibes to Edgewater, with tiki-inspired beers, Hawaiian music, an oyster bar, and tiki mugs across both brewery floors.

weekendEvents.title: STEM & Creative Core Showcase 2026
weekendEvents.description: A free open-house event at CSU Spur where Colorado students present tech projects and creative work across STEM and related fields, with hands-on demonstrations.
weekendEvents.location: CSU Spur, 4777 National Western Drive, Denver

topStory.headline: Trump's Rhetoric on Iran Marks Sharp Break From Traditional U.S. Foreign Policy Moral Framework
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump has escalated his rhetoric against Iran following recent military strikes, declaring on Truth Social that Iran had plans to "completely obliterate Israel" and that those plans are now "dead," while threatening to destroy Iran so thoroughly it could never "be built back, as a Nation, again" if it continues to block the Strait of Hormuz. Trump has also described Iranians as "a nation of terror and hate," though he later softened his language to call them "great people" who are "smart, brilliant, energetic."

The shift in tone represents a significant departure from the moral and democratic framing that prior U.S. administrations used to justify American foreign policy interventions abroad. Presidents including Ronald Reagan and George W. Bush framed U.S. power in terms of freedom, democracy, and humanitarian ideals — language that, whatever its relationship to actual policy outcomes, served to build international support and diplomatic legitimacy for American leadership.

Critics note that the U.S. has historically paired that moral language with programs such as USAID and the National Endowment for Democracy, which combined development assistance and pro-Western political organizing to extend American influence globally. Under Trump, USAID was largely dismantled through actions associated with Elon Musk's cost-cutting effort, and an attempt to defund the National Endowment for Democracy was only reversed by Congress, signaling a broader retreat from the soft-power infrastructure that underpinned postwar U.S. hegemony.

Analysts argue the rhetorical and institutional changes reflect a strategic recalculation in response to what Trump and his advisers view as a declining U.S. global position, substituting overt economic and military pressure for the ideological justifications that previously helped sustain American influence. The implications for U.S. alliances, international institutions, and the ongoing standoff with Iran remain significant, as the administration's approach removes much of the diplomatic architecture that historically accompanied American military and economic coercion.
